note_data.title: Extraction and corrosion of metals
keyPoints: Metals are extracted from their **ores** using reduction, electrolysis or displacement reactions depending on their position in the reactivity series
Carbon can only reduce metals **less reactive than carbon** - this includes iron, zinc, lead, and copper
Metals **more reactive than carbon** (like aluminium) must be extracted by **electrolysis** of molten compounds
**Rusting** requires both oxygen and water to be present - removing either prevents rusting completely
Rusting is the **oxidation of iron** to form hydrated iron(III) oxide (rust)
Prevention methods include **galvanising** (zinc coating), **painting**, **oiling**, or **alloying** to make stainless steel
**Sacrificial protection** uses a more reactive metal like zinc or magnesium to protect iron from rusting
Iron is extracted in the **blast furnace** using coke (carbon), limestone (calcium carbonate), and iron ore
**Displacement reactions** occur when a more reactive metal displaces a less reactive metal from its compound

definitions.definition: A naturally occurring compound of a metal that can be extracted economically
The removal of oxygen from a compound or the gain of electrons
The corrosion of iron in the presence of oxygen and water to form hydrated iron(III) oxide
Coating iron or steel with a layer of zinc to prevent rusting
Using a more reactive metal to protect a less reactive metal from corrosion

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give a method and explain why it would work for the specific situation

modelAnswer.question: Explain why zinc is used for galvanising iron and how it protects the iron from rusting.
modelAnswer.answer: Zinc is more reactive than iron. Zinc forms a protective layer/barrier that prevents oxygen and water from reaching the iron surface. If the zinc coating is scratched, the zinc still protects the iron by sacrificial protection because zinc is oxidised instead of iron.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Zinc is more reactive than iron
Mark 2: Zinc forms a protective barrier/layer
Mark 3: Prevents oxygen and water reaching iron
Mark 4: Sacrificial protection occurs if coating is damaged

commonMistakes: Never say 'rust needs air' — always say 'rust needs oxygen and water'
Students often confuse galvanising with painting - galvanising provides sacrificial protection even when damaged
Don't say 'carbon reduces all metals' - carbon can only reduce metals less reactive than itself
Never write 'iron oxide' for rust - it must be 'hydrated iron(III) oxide'
Students forget that electrolysis uses molten compounds, not aqueous solutions for metal extraction
examinerTips: Extraction methods appear frequently in Paper 2 - learn the reactivity series order
Rusting prevention questions often ask for two methods - give different types (barrier + sacrificial)
Always state both oxygen AND water are needed for rusting - stating just 'air' loses marks
Blast furnace questions may ask for equations - practice the carbon monoxide reduction step
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Blast furnace diagram showing raw materials entering at top (iron ore, coke, limestone), hot air blast entering sides, and products leaving at bottom (molten iron, slag). Label temperatures and key reactions at different levels.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q3 (4 marks) — they asked students to identify extraction methods for different metals based on their position in the reactivity series
N/D 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — multiple choice question on naming the process used to extract metals from their ores (reduction)
M/J 2024 P2 Q5b (3 marks) — students had to explain why carbon cannot be used to extract aluminum and suggest an alternative method
N/D 2023 P2 Q4a (2 marks) — question on identifying which metals can be extracted using carbon reduction
M/J 2022 P2 Q6c (5 marks) — comprehensive question on galvanizing as a method of rust prevention, including the process and how it works

workedExample.question: Iron can be extracted from iron ore in a blast furnace. Explain how carbon monoxide is formed in the blast furnace and how it extracts iron from iron oxide. Include balanced chemical equations in your answer. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: State that coke (carbon) burns in hot air to form carbon dioxide: C + O₂ → CO₂
Step 2: Explain that carbon dioxide reacts with more hot coke to form carbon monoxide: CO₂ + C → 2CO
Step 3: Show that carbon monoxide reduces iron oxide to iron: Fe₂O₃ + 3CO → 2Fe + 3CO₂
Step 4: Conclude that carbon monoxide acts as the reducing agent, removing oxygen from iron oxide
workedExample.answer: Coke (carbon) burns in hot air: C + O₂ → CO₂. The carbon dioxide then reacts with more hot coke to produce carbon monoxide: CO₂ + C → 2CO. Carbon monoxide acts as a reducing agent and extracts iron from iron oxide: Fe₂O₃ + 3CO → 2Fe + 3CO₂. The carbon monoxide removes oxygen from the iron oxide, leaving pure iron metal.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for formation of CO₂, 1 mark for formation of CO with equation, 1 mark for reduction equation, 1 mark for identifying CO as reducing agent, 1 mark for explaining oxygen removal, 1 mark for correct balanced equations

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused electrolysis with electrolytic reduction' — always specify that reactive metals like aluminum require electrolytic reduction, not just 'electrolysis'
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently wrote incomplete equations for metal extraction' — always balance chemical equations completely and include state symbols where appropriate

Examiner Report 2024: 'Candidates often listed rust prevention methods without explaining how they work' — when discussing galvanizing or painting, explain that they form a barrier preventing oxygen and water contact
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw a labeled diagram of a blast furnace showing: the three main raw materials entering at the top (iron ore, coke, limestone), the hot air blast entering near the bottom, the different temperature zones, the chemical reactions occurring at each level, and the products leaving (molten iron at bottom, slag removal, waste gases at top). Include arrows showing direction of materials and label key temperatures (1500°C at bottom, 200°C at top).
note_data.quickRecap: Metal extraction method depends on reactivity: unreactive metals found native, moderately reactive metals extracted by carbon reduction, highly reactive metals need electrolytic reduction. Rusting requires oxygen AND water - prevention methods create barriers (painting, oiling) or sacrificial protection (galvanizing). Carbon monoxide is the actual reducing agent in blast furnaces, not carbon itself.
